en.extract:
The Snares penguin (Eudyptes robustus; Māori: Pokotiwha), also known as the Snares crested penguin and the Snares Islands penguin, is a penguin from New Zealand. The species breeds on the Snares Islands, a group of islands off the southern coast of the South Island. It is a yellow-crested penguin, with a size of 50–70 cm (19.5–27.5 in) and a weight of 2.5–4 kg (5.5–8.8 lb). It has dark blue-black upper parts and white underparts. It has a bright yellow eyebrow-stripe which extends over the eye to form a drooping, bushy crest. It has bare pink skin at the base of its large red-brown bill.
The species nests in colonies ranging in size from around 10 nests to around 1200, under forest cover or the open. The main colonies are located on North East Island; other colonies are established on Broughton Island as well as the rocky Western Chain.
The Snares penguin's main prey is krill, supplemented by squid and small fish. The species is rated as vulnerable by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) as its breeding range is restricted to one small island group. The population is estimated at around 25,000 breeding pairs.


Taxonomy
Snares penguins were originally collected in 1874 and named atrata by Frederick Hutton. However, Hutton lost his sample at sea whilst drawing the bird before a full speciation could be identified. A description written by Hutton and an illustration done by Keulemans in Buller's A History of the Birds of New Zealand are evidence that this is the same penguin previously identified by Hutton.
However, there is some debate over the classification of this penguin, and Oliver was able to successfully propose that a few slight anatomical differences did exist between the Snares penguins and the early description and illustration, leading the Snares penguin to be called robustus instead of atratus.


Distribution and habitat
The Snares penguin is named after the place in which it breeds
—the Snares Islands. The Snares Islands are a small group of islands off the coast of southern New Zealand. Although little is known of their range and migration outside of the breeding season, it is not thought that they migrate far in the winters. Occasional sightings have occurred on the coasts of Tasmania, southern Australia, the Chatham Islands, Stewart Island, and the southern New Zealand mainland. There are approximately 25,000 living pairs of Snares penguins.
Snares penguins nest in dense colonies under the tree cover of the Olearia forests or on coastal rocks. To build a nest, the penguins dig up shallow holes in the ground and layer the bottom with grass, leaves, twigs, peat, or pebbles. A small rim of mud is added to the rim of the nest to raise it above ground level. Vegetation at nesting sites dies dues to the dense nesting activities, and the colony moves to a new nesting site. Colonies nesting near streams may have some advantage, as the stream provides water for drinking and bathing. However, it is not essential that nesting colonies are located near a stream and many are far from any stream.


Description

The Snares penguin is often compared to the Fiordland penguin (Eudyptes pachyrhynchus), which is related by the genus of crested penguins (Eudyptes). Snares penguins can be distinguished from Fiordland penguins by a patch of skin at the base of their beaks. The Snares penguin is similarly coloured to other species of penguins, having a black head, back and flippers with a white belly. A bright-yellow crest, beginning at the base of the bill, runs along the upper part of the head on both sides and ends at the back of the head. It has a thick reddish-brown beak, traced with light pink skin at the base. The eyes are generally described as a bright red-brown colour, but this colouration can vary somewhat between individuals and in different lighting. The colour patterns under the wings differ from individual to individual, so it is not a good characteristic for species identification. 
The penguin can make a large variety of vocal noises. It is difficult to verbally describe these noises, but they range from hisses and explosive cries when threatened to rhythmical braying and trumpeting sounds that can be heard from long distances at sea.


Behaviour


Diet
The diet of Snares penguins usually consists of krill of the species Nyctiphanes australis, small fish (including codlings), and squid such as Nototodarus sloanii and Moroteuthopsis ingens. One study found that about 60 percent of the mass of stomach contents from Snares penguins consisted of krill, 30 percent was fish, and about 10 percent was cephalopods. The researchers concluded that the number of fish otoliths and cephalopod beaks indicated the importance of these types of prey to adult penguins while at sea. Another study reports the diet consisting of about 55 percent krill, 24 percent fish, and 21 percent cephalopods, also suggesting that fish and cephalopods are more important prey types than the percentages of stomach composition indicate.


Foraging ecology

The foraging patterns of breeding Snares penguins are well defined. After shared incubation of eggs, males leave on two-week-long foraging trips, which has been found to be synchronised with spring plankton blooms, as they are reliable predictors of food sources. Upon the return of the males, the females go on somewhat shorter foraging trips (less than a week), returning in time for the chicks to hatch. Throughout the chick guard stage, the female is the lone provider of food performing short foraging trips (one to three days). While male penguins performing long foraging trips reach depths of up to 120 m, chick rearing birds usually exhibit shallow pursuit diving strategies (average dive depths 20–40 m) to catch prey.


Predators
Predators of adult Snares penguins are sea lions and leopard seals. Their eggs and chicks are vulnerable to predation by skuas and petrels.


Courtship and breeding
The breeding season of Snares penguins occurs in the summer of New Zealand, beginning in early September and ending in late January. Snares penguins begin reproducing in the age range of 5 to 9 years old. To court females, single males stand erect with their wings spread out and pumping their chest. It is likely that the pairs remain faithful to each other for more than one season. The pairs then build a nest, most of the work being done by the male. The female lays two eggs, typically about three to five days apart, which hatch 31–37 days later. The second laid egg is up to 85 percent larger than the first and also hatches first. The smaller, later-hatched chick is outcompeted by its siblings and usually does not survive, also called "siblicide". Egg-size dimorphism is particularly large in Snares penguins, and the larger egg hatching first only accentuates the sibling size difference.
Hatching pattern and size-dimorphic eggs are two of the mechanisms by which birds can change their breeding pattern in response to environmental and breeding conditions. In altricial birds, such as Snares penguins, egg-size variation and asynchronous hatching are adaptive mechanisms that may lead to brood reduction. However, Snares penguins and other members of their genus, Eudyptes, differ from most other birds in that their last laid egg is larger and hatches first rather than being the smallest and last to hatch.
Egg incubation does not occur until both eggs have been laid, and they are usually incubated one in front of the other. The smaller, first-laid egg is often placed in the anterior position for incubation, which is thought to be the less favourable location since they have lower and more variable temperatures during incubation. Although the smaller, first egg receives less favourable incubation, it was found that egg-size dimorphism affects the hatching pattern in Snares penguins instead of egg incubation position. Both parents incubate the eggs. For the first ten days, they alternate. Then the male leaves for an extended period (about 12 days) to forage for food, while the mother stays with the eggs. When he returns, the roles are reversed. Also, upon his return, the pair engages in a display of bowing and trumpeting, which further strengthens their bond.
For the first three weeks after hatching, the male guards the chicks from predators while the female searches for food, returning each day to feed her offspring. It was observed that parents can recognise their chicks and chicks can recognise their parents. The study found that calls between chick and parent were more important for recognition than visual cues. The parents provide food for their young each day until it has moulted and can forage for its own food. The chicks fledge at approximately 11 weeks.


Conservation
Although Snares penguins are not currently threatened, they are considered a vulnerable species. If a threat arose, it could quickly wipe out their population because their breeding grounds are confined to a small island group. Possible threats include the introduction of a new predator, overfishing around the islands that would deplete their food source, increasing water temperatures from global warming driving prey away, and pollution. The New Zealand government has taken action to protect the Snares Islands habitats and marine feeding grounds.


References


Further reading
Wilson, David. "Causes and Benefits of Chick Aggregations in Penguins." The Auk 126.3 (2009): 688–93. JSTOR. Web. 16 April 2011.


External links

State of Penguins: Snares penguin – detailed and current species account of (Eudyptes robustus)
Snares penguin on PenguinWorld
Snares penguins from International Penguin Conservation website
ARKive - images and movies of the snares crested penguin (Eudyptes robustus)
www.pinguins.info information about all species of penguins
TerraNature - Snares crested penguin, images and information on six New Zealand penguins

{{Speciesbox
| image = Snares Penguin, Southland, NZ imported from iNaturalist photo 347215306.jpg
| status = VU
| status_system = IUCN3.1
| status_ref = <ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2018 |title=''Eudyptes robustus'' |volume=2018 |article-number=e.T22697782A132602343 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T22697782A132602343.en |access-date=11 November 2021}}</ref>

The '''Snares penguin''' ('''''Eudyptes robustus'''''; {{langx|mi|Pokotiwha}}),<ref>{{Cite web |title=Snares crested penguin {{!}} Pokotiwha {{!}} New Zealand Birds Online |url=https://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/snares-crested-penguin-pokotiwha |access-date=2023-11-26 |website=nzbirdsonline.org.nz |language=en}}</ref> also known as the '''Snares crested penguin''' and the '''Snares Islands penguin''', is a [[penguin]] from [[New Zealand]]. The species breeds on the [[Snares Islands / Tini Heke|Snares Islands]], a group of islands off the southern coast of the [[South Island]]. It is a yellow-crested penguin, with a size of {{convert|50|–|70|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} and a weight of {{convert|2.5|–|4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. It has dark blue-black upper parts and white underparts. It has a bright yellow eyebrow-stripe which extends over the eye to form a drooping, bushy crest. It has bare pink skin at the base of its large red-brown bill.

The species nests in colonies ranging in size from around 10 nests to around 1200, under forest cover or the open. The main colonies are located on [[North East Island, New Zealand|North East Island]]; other colonies are established on [[Broughton Island, New Zealand|Broughton Island]] as well as the rocky [[Western Chain, New Zealand|Western Chain]].

The Snares penguin's main prey is [[krill]], supplemented by [[squid]] and small fish. The species is rated as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) as its breeding range is restricted to one small island group. The population is estimated at around 25,000 breeding pairs.

== Taxonomy ==
Snares penguins were originally collected in 1874 and named ''atrata'' by [[Frederick Hutton (scientist)|Frederick Hutton]]. However, Hutton lost his sample at sea whilst drawing the bird before a full [[speciation]] could be identified. A description written by Hutton and an illustration done by [[John Gerrard Keulemans|Keulemans]] in [[Walter Buller|Buller's]] ''A History of the Birds of New Zealand'' are evidence that this is the same penguin previously identified by Hutton.

However, there is some debate over the classification of this penguin, and Oliver was able to successfully propose that a few slight anatomical differences did exist between the Snares penguins and the early description and illustration, leading the Snares penguin to be called ''robustus'' instead of ''atratus''.<ref name="Warham">Warham, John. "The Breeding Biology and Behavior of the Snares Crested Penguin." Journal of the Royal Society of New Zealand 4.1 (1974): 63-108. Google Books. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Snares penguin is named after the place in which it breeds
—the Snares Islands. The Snares Islands are a small group of islands off the coast of southern New Zealand. Although little is known of their range and [[Animal migration|migration]] outside of the breeding season, it is not thought that they migrate far in the winters. Occasional sightings have occurred on the coasts of [[Tasmania]], southern Australia, the [[Chatham Islands]], [[Stewart Island]], and the southern New Zealand mainland.<ref name="ARKive">{{cite web
 |title        = Snares Crested Penguin - Eudyptes Robustus
 |publisher    = ARKive.org
 |url          = http://www.arkive.org/snares-crested-penguin/eudyptes-robustus/video-00.html#text=Facts
 |access-date   = 1 December 2011
 |archive-url  = https://web.archive.org/web/20121007042717/http://www.arkive.org/snares-crested-penguin/eudyptes-robustus/video-00.html#text=Facts
 |archive-date = 7 October 2012
 }}</ref><ref name="Center_for_Biological_Diversity" /> There are approximately 25,000 living pairs of Snares penguins.<ref name="Antarctic_Connection">{{cite web
|title=Snares Island Penguin
|work=Antarctic Connection
|url=http://www.antarcticconnection.com/shopcontent.asp?type=wildlife-penguins-snares
|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111017131129/http://www.antarcticconnection.com/shopcontent.asp?type=wildlife-penguins-snares
|url-status=usurped
|archive-date=17 October 2011
|access-date=1 December 2011}}</ref>

Snares penguins nest in dense colonies under the tree cover of the Olearia forests or on coastal rocks.<ref name="PenguinWorld" /> To build a nest, the penguins dig up shallow holes in the ground and layer the bottom with grass, leaves, twigs, peat, or pebbles.<ref name="Antarctic_Connection" /> A small rim of mud is added to the rim of the nest to raise it above ground level.<ref name="Warham" /> Vegetation at nesting sites dies dues to the dense nesting activities, and the colony moves to a new nesting site.<ref name="ARKive" /> Colonies nesting near streams may have some advantage, as the stream provides water for drinking and bathing. However, it is not essential that nesting colonies are located near a stream and many are far from any stream.<ref name="Warham" />

== Description ==
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Snares Penguin, Southland, NZ-SO, NZ imported from iNaturalist photo 462871455.jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|crop_right_perc=45|crop_left_perc=5|crop_top_perc=15|caption=Snares penguin}}
The Snares penguin is often compared to the [[Fiordland penguin]] (''Eudyptes pachyrhynchus''), which is related by the genus of [[crested penguin]]s (''Eudyptes''). Snares penguins can be distinguished from Fiordland penguins by a patch of skin at the base of their beaks.<ref name="MarineBio">{{cite web
|title=Snares Penguins, Eudyptes robustus
|publisher=MarineBio.org
|url=http://marinebio.org/species.asp?id=657
|access-date=1 December 2011}}</ref> The Snares penguin is similarly coloured to other species of penguins, having a black head, back and flippers with a white belly. A bright-yellow crest, beginning at the base of the bill, runs along the upper part of the head on both sides and ends at the back of the head. It has a thick reddish-brown beak, traced with light pink skin at the base.<ref name="Antarctic_Connection"/> The eyes are generally described as a bright red-brown colour, but this colouration can vary somewhat between individuals and in different lighting.<ref name="Warham"/> The colour patterns under the wings differ from individual to individual, so it is not a good characteristic for species identification.<ref name="PenguinWorld">{{cite web
|title=Snares Penguin: Eudyptes Robustus
|publisher=PenguinWorld.com
|url=http://www.penguinworld.com/types/snares.html
|access-date=1 December 2011}}</ref> 
	
The penguin can make a large variety of vocal noises. It is difficult to verbally describe these noises, but they range from hisses and explosive cries when threatened to rhythmical braying and trumpeting sounds that can be heard from long distances at sea.<ref name="Warham"/>

== Behaviour ==

===Diet===
The diet of Snares penguins usually consists of krill of the species ''[[Nyctiphanes]] australis'', small fish (including [[Moridae|codlings]]), and squid such as ''[[Nototodarus sloanii]]'' and ''[[Moroteuthopsis ingens]]''.<ref name="Williams1995">{{cite book |last=Williams |first=Tony D. |title=The Penguins: Spheniscidae |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1995 |pages=200-206}}</ref> One study found that about 60 percent of the mass of stomach contents from Snares penguins consisted of krill, 30 percent was fish, and about 10 percent was cephalopods. The researchers concluded that the number of fish [[otolith]]s and cephalopod beaks indicated the importance of these types of prey to adult penguins while at sea.<ref>Mattern T, Houston D, Lalas C, Setiawan A, and Davis LS (2009) "Diet Composition, Continuity in Prey Availability and Marine Habitat - Keystones to Population Stability in the Snares Penguin (Eudyptes Robustus)". Emu 109(3): 204-13.</ref> Another study reports the diet consisting of about 55 percent krill, 24 percent fish, and 21 percent cephalopods, also suggesting that fish and cephalopods are more important prey types than the percentages of stomach composition indicate.<ref name="Mattern">Mattern, Thomas. "Marine Ecology of Offshore and Inshore Foraging Penguins: the Snares Penguin Eudyptes Robustus and Yellow-eyed Penguin Megadyptes Antipodes." Australasian Digital Theses Program (2007). University of Otago. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref>

===Foraging ecology===
[[File:Diving Snares Penguin.jpg|thumb|Diving Snares penguins]]{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Snares Penguin, Southland, NZ imported from iNaturalist photo 355810804.jpg|caption=Swimming|desired_width=250|align=right|crop_right_perc=30|crop_left_perc=25|crop_top_perc=30|crop_bottom_perc=20}}
[[File:Snares Penguin, Southland, NZ imported from iNaturalist photo 355810907.jpg|thumb|With crest standing erect, [[North East Island, New Zealand|North East Island]]]]
The foraging patterns of breeding Snares penguins are well defined. After shared incubation of eggs, males leave on two-week-long foraging trips, which has been found to be synchronised with spring [[plankton]] blooms, as they are reliable predictors of food sources.<ref name="Mattern"/> Upon the return of the males, the females go on somewhat shorter foraging trips (less than a week), returning in time for the chicks to hatch. Throughout the chick guard stage, the female is the lone provider of food performing short foraging trips (one to three days). While male penguins performing long foraging trips reach depths of up to 120 m, chick rearing birds usually exhibit shallow pursuit diving strategies (average dive depths 20–40 m) to catch prey.<ref name="Mattern"/>

===Predators===
Predators of adult Snares penguins are [[sea lion]]s and [[leopard seal]]s. Their eggs and chicks are vulnerable to predation by [[skua]]s and [[petrel]]s.<ref name="MarineBio"/>

=== Courtship and breeding ===
The breeding season of Snares penguins occurs in the summer of New Zealand, beginning in early September and ending in late January.<ref name="Massaro_Davis">Massaro, Melanie, and Lloyd S. Davis. "Differences in Egg Size, Shell Thickness, Pore Density, Pore Diameter and Water Vapour Conductance between First and Second Eggs of Snares Penguins Eudyptes Robustus and Their Influence on Hatching Asynchrony." Ibis 147.2 (2005): 251-58. Wiley Online Library. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref> Snares penguins begin reproducing in the age range of 5 to 9 years old.<ref name="Warham"/> To court females, single males stand erect with their wings spread out and pumping their chest.<ref name="ARKive"/> It is likely that the pairs remain faithful to each other for more than one season. The pairs then build a nest, most of the work being done by the male.<ref name="Warham"/> The female lays two eggs, typically about three to five days apart, which hatch 31–37 days later. The second laid egg is up to 85 percent larger than the first and also hatches first. The smaller, later-hatched chick is outcompeted by its siblings and usually does not survive, also called "[[siblicide]]". Egg-size dimorphism is particularly large in Snares penguins, and the larger egg hatching first only accentuates the sibling size difference.<ref name="Massaro_Davis"/>

Hatching pattern and size-dimorphic eggs are two of the mechanisms by which birds can change their breeding pattern in response to environmental and breeding conditions. In [[altricial]] birds, such as Snares penguins, egg-size variation and asynchronous hatching are adaptive mechanisms that may lead to brood reduction. However, Snares penguins and other members of their genus, ''Eudyptes'', differ from most other birds in that their last laid egg is larger and hatches first rather than being the smallest and last to hatch.<ref>[[Tore Slagsvold|Slagsvold, Tore]], Jostein Sandvik, Gunnar Rofstad, Oystein Lorensten, and Magne Husby. "On the Adaptive Value of Intraclutch Egg-Size Variation in Birds." The Auk 101.4 (1984): 685-97. JSTOR. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref>

[[Egg incubation]] does not occur until both eggs have been laid, and they are usually incubated one in front of the other. The smaller, first-laid egg is often placed in the anterior position for incubation, which is thought to be the less favourable location since they have lower and more variable temperatures during incubation. Although the smaller, first egg receives less favourable incubation, it was found that egg-size dimorphism affects the hatching pattern in Snares penguins instead of egg incubation position.<ref>Massaro, Melanie, and LloydS. Davis. "Preferential Incubation Positions for Different Sized Eggs and Their Influence on Incubation Period and Hatching Asynchrony in Snares Crested (Eudyptes Robustus) and Yellow-eyed Penguins (Megadyptes Antipodes)." Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology 56.5 (2004): 426-34. JSTOR. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref> Both parents incubate the eggs. For the first ten days, they alternate. Then the male leaves for an extended period (about 12 days) to forage for food, while the mother stays with the eggs. When he returns, the roles are reversed. Also, upon his return, the pair engages in a display of bowing and trumpeting, which further strengthens their bond.

For the first three weeks after hatching, the male guards the chicks from predators while the female searches for food, returning each day to feed her offspring.<ref name="ARKive"/> It was observed that parents can recognise their chicks and chicks can recognise their parents. The study found that calls between chick and parent were more important for recognition than visual cues.<ref>Proffitt, Fiona, and Ian McLean. "Recognition of Parents' Calls by Chicks of the Snares Crested Penguin." Bird Behavior 9 (1990): 103-13. Ingentaconnect. Web. 16 April 2011.</ref> The parents provide food for their young each day until it has [[moult]]ed and can forage for its own food.<ref name="Antarctic_Connection"/> The chicks [[fledge]] at approximately 11 weeks.<ref name="MarineBio"/>

{{Clear}}

== Conservation ==
Although Snares penguins are not currently threatened, they are considered a vulnerable species. If a threat arose, it could quickly wipe out their population because their breeding grounds are confined to a small island group. Possible threats include the introduction of a new predator, [[overfishing]] around the islands that would deplete their food source, increasing water temperatures from [[global warming]] driving prey away, and pollution.<ref name="ARKive" /><ref name="Center_for_Biological_Diversity">{{cite web
|title=Snares Crested Penguin
|work=Center for Biological Diversity
|url=http://www.biologicaldiversity.org/species/birds/penguins/Snares_crested_penguin.html
|access-date=1 December 2011}}</ref> The New Zealand government has taken action to protect the Snares Islands habitats and marine feeding grounds.<ref name="Antarctic_Connection" />

== References ==

== Further reading ==

zh.extract:
斯岛黄眉企鹅（台湾作誘惑企鵝，学名：Eudyptes robustus），是企鹅目企鹅科的一种鸟类。
斯岛黄眉企鹅体重约3038.02克，翼长约97.8毫米，嘴峰长约56.1毫米，喙宽度约16毫米，喙厚度约25.7毫米，跗蹠长约28毫米，尾长约84.5毫米。斯岛黄眉企鹅是部分迁徙的鸟类，其栖息在开放的海洋及海岛环境中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是水生动物。
斯岛黄眉企鹅分布于斯奈爾斯群島和新西兰附近水域。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。


参考资料
